hook: You already know how to solve two equations with two unknowns — but what happens when an engineer faces 500 equations with 500 unknowns? This unit builds the language and machinery to handle linear systems of any size, revealing that the same ideas scale beautifully. By the end, you'll see how a simple question like 'does this system have a solution?' connects geometry, algebra, and computation in one elegant framework.
concept: The unifying idea is that a system of linear equations, a geometric question about vectors, and a matrix multiplication are all the same problem in different clothing — formally captured by the matrix equation $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to set up and interpret systems of linear equations, identifying when they have one solution, infinitely many, or none at all.
Master the row reduction algorithm to systematically simplify any linear system into echelon form, making solutions straightforward to read off.
Reinterpret linear systems as questions about vectors, and determine whether a target vector lies within the span of a given set of vectors.
Unify systems of equations and vector equations into the single compact form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, and use matrix-vector multiplication fluently.
Describe the complete solution set of $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ in parametric vector form, identifying which variables are free and how they generate the solution space.
Apply linear systems to real-world problems such as network flow, balancing chemical equations, and economic models to see the direct impact of the theory.
Determine whether a set of vectors is linearly independent, and understand why this property is the key to knowing when a system's solution is unique.
